data.title: MongoDB: Sandbagging Earnings (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The last time we discussed MongoDB Inc. (MDB) "MDB," it was March and the company just reported earnings. While the earnings were solid, the guidance was disappointing, and the stock fell hard. I rated the stock a hold then, as it seemed the upside potential was limited after reporting poor guidance. Also, the post-liberation day smackdown caused MDB's stock to crater by another 30%, before MDB finally bottomed around the $140 level.

MDB 1-Year Chart

MDB recently reported earnings and the results were actually really good, especially when compared with sandbagged guidance last quarter. In fact, it seems that MDB likely lowballed guidance considerably due to increased uncertainty last quarter.

For Q1, MDB beat both top and bottom-line estimates, but perhaps most importantly, MDB guided higher. Due to the better than anticipated results, and the stock still being well off its highs, I am upgrading MDB's stock to a buy from my hold rating. I am also increasing my 2025 year-end price target from $250 to $285.

MDB's Excellent Earnings Announcement

MongoDB reported Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $1.00, beating the consensus estimate by $0.34, smashing the consensus estimate by over 50%. Revenue came in at $549M, a $21.5M beat, and a considerable 22% YoY increase.

These results are fascinating, because MDB guided to only $524-529M in sales for Q1 around three months ago. So, MDB beat its own guidance by over 4%, which is very significant, illustrating that MDB likely sandbagged guidance due to limited visibility and tariff related uncertainty.

While I believed MDB sandbagged guidance in the previous quarter, I still had to go with a hold rating because I had no way to prove the company deliberately downplayed its growth and earnings capacity because of the transitory challenges in the economic landscape.

Nonetheless, here we are, and MDB is doing considerably better than perceived. We talked about sales, but look at MDB's earnings. The company guided to an EPS mid-point of roughly $0.65 per share. Yet, it delivered EPS of $1, smashing the consensus EPS estimate and its own lowballed guidance by about 50%.

Additionally, MDB increased its buyback program to $1 billion, and offered solid Q2 guidance. The visibility is clearly much better here for MDB than it was several months ago.

MDB's Very Solid Q2 and Full-Year Guidance

The company offered a high-end estimate of $553M for Q2 and $2.29B for the year, suggesting it could surpass the consensus estimates. However, the real progress appears to be on the earnings side, as MDB guided to a Q2 mid-point of $0.64 vs. the consensus figure of just $0.58. Additionally, for the full year, MDB plans to deliver about $3.05 on EPS this year, vs. the lowballed consensus figure of just $2.65.

MDB's Valuation Still Appears Attractive Here

MDB has a remarkable history of beating consensus estimates. In fact, I was quite surprised when doing the research here, as most high-quality mega-cap tech stocks don't even have the track record MDB has achieved.

MDB has not missed on earnings or EPS estimates in at least the last five years. MDB's TTM consensus EPS estimate was only about $2.50. However MDB delivered $4.14 instead, illustrating a very considerable 65% EPS outperformance rate.

Now, with this kind of consistent track record, there is no reason to believe that MDB's outperformance will suddenly stop. No, instead, the company can continue to outperform, in my view.

Next year's consensus EPS estimate is only about $3.69. However, this number is likely lowballed, and the higher end EPS estimates range to around $5.50, and I think we can see the estimates lifted as we advance.

The Bottom-Line

As MDB delivered $4.14, it may not have a problem delivering $5.50 in EPS next year. Given that MDB still trades around $225, its forward P/E ratio may be about 40 here, which is relatively inexpensive for an AI software company growing at a very fast pace.

On a forward P/S basis, MDB has a ratio of about 7, also relatively inexpensive for a solid AI company in this market. So, the valuation seems attractive here, and there is a high probability for positive EPS and sales growth revisions after these excellent results.

Due to the solid performance, MDB is a buy again, in my view, and I lifted the year-end price target to $285, representing about a 27% upside potential. We could also see this stock appreciate more considerably in a bullish case outcome.

Risks To MDB

MDB faces completion from Microsoft, Amazon, and other tech giants as well as smaller players in the space. Therefore, competition is a serious threat. Additionally, MDB faces risks due to the potential for a slower than anticipated economic atmosphere. Tariffs and other destabilizing factors could also impact MDB's stock negatively. MDB also faces company-specific challenges like execution risks and other elements. Investors should consider these and other risks before investing in MDB's stock.

data.title: TotalEnergies: Undervalued, Underappreciated, But Positioned To Outperform
data.description:
TotalEnergies SE (NYSE:TTE) is a global energy company which seems to be neglected by the market, despite its rare combination of undervaluation, operational resilience and strategic clarity. With double-digit shareholder returns, disciplined capital allocation and a credible path to low-carbon profitability, the company is positioning itself as a European super major that not only manages the energy transition, but monetizes it. This article examines whether the difference between market pricing and business fundamentals creates a compelling entry point for long-term investors.

Company Fundamentals and Positioning

TotalEnergies operates with a model that spans the entire energy value chain from upstream oil and gas exploration to downstream refining and petrochemicals, as well as marketing, trading and increasingly, power generation and energy storage. These five activities give an operational flexibility and cash flow stability. When refining margins shrink, upstream profits can compensate; when oil prices fall, trading or power generation activities can compensate. This diversification enables the company to maintain a high level of profitability throughout the commodity cycles.

Although roughly 80% of its revenues still come from oil and gas, but the company is actively pivoting. The Integrated Power division, which includes renewable electricity generation, storage, and trading, already accounts for approximately 8% of group turnover and is expected to reach 15-20% by 2030. This unit is expected to generate a return on average capital employed (ROACE) above 12% by the end of the decade, a target aligned with the Group's fossil assets. That ambition is not theoretical, and it is already backed by a pipeline of over 35 GW in committed capacity, across solar, wind, and energy storage platforms and Its target 100 TWh of green electricity by 2030, up from 33 TWh today.

TotalEnergies is present all over the world by starting with Africa and Middle East where the company leverages cost-effective production in long-standing partnerships in Nigeria, Angola, Mozambique, Iraq, and the UAE. In Europe, it maintains strategic dominance across refining, distribution, and storage infrastructure, while in North America the company is scaling LNG capabilities via partnerships in NextDecade and Cameron LNG, and expanding renewables through acquisitions like Clearway and offshore wind projects along the U.S. coasts. Due to this geographic repartition, the Group is able to mitigate geopolitical risks by reallocating output flexibly, and diversifying exposure across currencies and demand profiles.

In liquefied natural gas (LNG), TotalEnergies holds approximately 10% of the global market share. With a fully integrated model from upstream production (Mozambique LNG, Papua LNG), to liquefaction infrastructure (Cameron, Qatar North Field), to proprietary shipping and customer delivery, it makes a reliable long-term supplier to both Asia and Europe. Today, LNG has become a strategic growth vector, benefiting from structural demand post-2022 and serving as a transitional fuel aligned with decarbonization policies.

On the oil side, The Group holds exploration permits for strategic reserves, which include Lake Albert in Uganda and the Suriname offshore basin. The proven reserves from these locations are sufficient for eleven years of production at current levels with upstream production costs remaining under $5 per barrel, far below those of many independent producers, reinforcing its cost leadership.

TotalEnergies has achieved a moderate yet steady growth with revenue increasing from $176 billion to $237 billion between 2019 and 2023 which represents a 7.7% compound annual growth rate (CAGR) over the period and despite significant challenges with the pandemic, supply shocks, and commodity volatility. For 2024, revenue remained above $200 billion, down 10.6% year-over-year due to weaker hydrocarbon prices and refining margins. And for the first quarter of 2025 revenue was $47.9 billion, indicating an 8% annual decline year -over- year under comparable circumstances, but for me, these figures clearly reflect the Group's resilience exposure to macroeconomic cycles.

Contrary to U.S. peers like ExxonMobil or Chevron, which are still heavily fossil-fuel dependent, TotalEnergies stands out with one of the most credible and capital-backed energy transition strategies among supermajors. The Group is already executing this shift through large-scale projects in offshore wind (UK, South Korea), solar (India, Texas, Spain), and emerging hydrogen and storage initiatives. This dual focus enables TotalEnergies to leverage hydrocarbon profitability while building long-term clean energy optionality.

Financial Quality and Valuation

In a less favorable environment than 2022-2023, the Group continues to demonstrate financial discipline and generate strong free cash flow. For the first quarter of 2025, adjusted net income reached $4.2 billion, down 18% from the previous year due to lower oil prices, margin compression and seasonal slowdown in the gas segment. However, operating cash flow saw an 18% increase, reaching $2.56 billion due to an enhanced working capital dynamics and resilience across the business. And the Free cash flow after organic investments was $2.5 billion, which was sufficient to fully fund the quarterly dividend and the first $2 billion tranche of the share repurchase program without increasing debt or compromising allocations for future growth.

Net debt rose to $20.1 billion, lifting the net debt/EBITDA ratio to 1.46x, a level still well within comfort zones and largely the result of proactive shareholder returns rather than operational strain. Profitability remains strong, with ROE at 11.7%, ROIC at 8.4%, and a 13.1% free cash flow yield, placing the Group among the most cash-productive names in its peer set.

This financial strength translates into a sustainable and attractive shareholder return profile. Indeed, the current dividend yield of ~5.5% is backed by stable LNG earnings and conservative capex planning. The 5% dividend hike in early 2025 reflects a deliberate long-term policy, not a short-term windfall. It complements an $8 billion buyback program executed in 2024, signaling management's intent to return surplus capital while preserving strategic flexibility. In a sector often defined by volatility, TotalEnergies offers a rare combination: resilient income, disciplined reinvestment, and credible growth optionality.

TotalEnergies continues to trade at significantly lower multiples than its main peers, despite comparable margins and cash flow. The stock pays around 9.3 times 2024 earnings, compared with 13.7 times for ExxonMobil, 15.9 times for Chevron and over 14 times for Shell. On an EV/EBITDA basis, the multiple of 4.4 times is also well below that of the US majors (over 6.5), and in line with Shell, which shares a more integrated approach and comparable energy diversification. The market therefore seems to be applying a structural discount to TotalEnergies, a discount that can be explained neither by operational underperformance nor by excessive debt nor even by lower profitability. Rather, it seems to reflect a geographical and regulatory bias against the European majors, as well as persistent skepticism about the transition to low-carbon energies.

To complement the relative valuation, I built a five-year DCF model based on a Free Cash Flow to the Firm (FCFF) approach, anchored in a Brent price scenario of $65. The assumptions reflect a prudent yet realistic framework: a stable EBIT margin around 10%, annual capex maintained at $17 billion in line with the company's long-term investment guidance, and a discount rate (WACC) of 7.5% to capture both geopolitical and sectoral risks. Depreciation and amortization were set conservatively at $13 billion per year, and a 25% effective tax rate was applied to reflect TotalEnergies' global exposure. Using a perpetual growth rate of 1.5% beyond 2030, the model yields an enterprise value of $186 billion and an equity value of approximately $184.6 billion. With 2.3 billion shares outstanding, the resulting intrinsic value stands at $80.26 per share, implying a 34% upside from current market levels. This valuation reinforces the view that the stock is significantly undervalued even under cautious assumptions, and that the market is not fully pricing in the long-term earnings stability and cash generation capacity of the group in its division.

Catalysts and Risks

It's important to take in consideration some catalyst in the investment case of TotalEnergies over the short-medium term. The first and perhaps most impactful catalyst is the continued global build-out of the Group's liquefied natural gas (LNG) platform, which has become increasingly strategic since 2022 in light of geopolitical realignment and Europe's accelerated shift away from Russian pipeline gas.

The company is involved in several cornerstone LNG projects that already contributed and will continue to contribute meaningfully to earnings and free cash flow from 2026 onwards firstly with Mozambique LNG project, which represents a capacity of 13 million tonnes per annum (MTPA), which is gradually being reactivated after a prolonged halt due to regional security concerns. In the United States, the Cameron LNG Phase 2 expansion co-financed with Sempra Energy, will add export optionality at the heart of the global Atlantic basin gas trade, and it's important to remember that, in 2023, TotalEnergies signed a 20-year offtake agreement for 1,5 MTPA from the Rio Grande LNG project in Texas, further anchoring its position as a long-term supplier to Europe and Asia. The Group has also secured 6.3 MTPA through its participation in Qatar's North Field East megaproject, with deliveries set to begin in 2026. Moreover, all these projects are structured around long-term contracts indexed to Henry Hub prices plus spreads, which ensure a visible and recurring cash flow profile that enhances balance sheet resilience. In addition to that, natural gas is experiencing strong structural demand growth in Asia, particularly in fast-developing economies such as India, Vietnam, and the Philippines, where it serves as a transitional fuel within decarbonization roadmaps. For me, these elements support the strategic rationale behind TotalEnergies' LNG build out and provides a long demand tail for its future cargoes.

The second major catalyst is the scaling of the Integrated Power division, which now sits at the heart of the Group's diversification strategy. Indeed, TotalEnergies aims to become a global utility-scale player with a target of producing 100 terawatt-hours of electricity annually by 2030, a level comparable to leading European utilities such as Iberdrola or Enel. The Group has publicly stated its ambition to deliver a return on average capital employed (ROACE) above 12% for this business line by the end of the decade, a level significantly above traditional utility returns. If achieved, such performance would likely trigger a re-evaluation of the stock's valuation framework, gradually closing the gap between oil-centric and hybrid energy models and allowing TotalEnergies to earn valuation multiples more consistent with integrated infrastructure companies.

The third thematic catalyst is the normalization of macroeconomic and oil market conditions. Today, TotalEnergies still trades at a visible discount to U.S. peers, a gap that is less about fundamentals and more about market perception. Factors such as sluggish Chinese growth, oil price volatility, and a general skepticism toward European energy majors have contributed to this valuation gap. Yet, several developments could begin to close it. A stabilization in real interest rates could revalue long-duration infrastructure assets, particularly in the renewables space. A Brent Crude price anchored in the $70 to $80 range would mechanically support free cash flow, especially given the Group's low upstream breakeven. And a reduction in tensions between OPEC and the U.S. would ease political risk premiums currently embedded in sector valuations. Under such a central macro scenario, the persistent valuation discount applied to TotalEnergies relative to ExxonMobil or Chevron would appear increasingly unwarranted.

However, alongside these catalysts, TotalEnergies remains exposed to several key risks and the first is commodity price risk. Even despite its diversification, TotalEnergies remains exposed to oil and gas price cycles. And a sustained Brent price below $65 per barrel would materially compress upstream earnings and put pressure on free cash flow, even if some of the shock is absorbed by the more resilient LNG and Power divisions. Volatility in LNG spot prices, particularly in Asia, also remains a challenge. But long-term contracts mitigate this to some extent even if the Group is not immune to periods of sharp price dislocation, especially if global gas balances tighten or regional bottlenecks persist. While the Group's integration and hedging help buffer these effects, a prolonged energy downturn is not impossible, and it could slow deleveraging, delay buybacks, or constrain growth capex.

The second area of risk, is the geopolitical uncertainty and project execution with key growth projects such as Mozambique LNG, which remain dependent on the stabilization of the security situation in Cabo Delgado. It's important to remember that the Group's historical exposure to Russia, notably through its participation in Arctic LNG 2, has already resulted in a $4.1 billion impairment charge and illustrates the vulnerability of long-cycle projects to sudden geopolitical changes. In addition, future upstream projects in regions such as Iraq, Nigeria and Suriname could face operational delays, expropriation risks or cost overruns which could have setbacks in these jurisdictions could have a significant impact on production schedules and cash flow forecasts and even more if several delays occur simultaneously.

The third area of concern relates to the regulatory and ESG framework. As a major European group, TotalEnergies is increasingly exposed to tougher climate policy. The EU's "Fit for 55" package, carbon border adjustment mechanisms, and stricter emissions targets could drive up compliance costs in its refining, petrochemicals, and electricity businesses. In particular, EU carbon taxes and sustainable aviation fuel requirements could reduce margins or divert investment away from Europe. At the same time, ESG-focused capital flows continue to penalize large oil companies, regardless of their transition efforts. Several institutional investors continue to apply blanket exclusions to fossil fuel companies, limiting additional demand for their shares and potentially weighing on their valuation multiples, even when their fundamentals improve.

And lastly, TotalEnergies' energy transition strategy, while credible and forward-looking, carries significant execution risk. The development of large-scale offshore wind, solar farms, and storage infrastructure is capital intensive and operationally complex. If the Integrated Power division fails to achieve its ROACE targets or suffers delays due to permitting issues, inflationary pressures, or supply chain constraints, the market could lose confidence in its ability to deliver returns comparable to its legacy businesses. This, in turn, would reinforce the valuation discount and reframe the Power segment as a dilutive diversification rather than a value-enhancing one.

Conclusion

Under current conditions, TotalEnergies offers one of the most asymmetric risk/return profiles among large global energy companies. The Group has built a diversified and capital-efficient energy model that is resilient, forward-looking and increasingly cash-generative. If the company continues to implement its strategy in its two pillars, fossil energy and renewable energy, and if macroeconomic conditions remain stable overall, I believe it would be normal to see TotalEnergies' share price rise significantly over the next 6 to 12 months to $70 minimum even if oil and LNG prices remain at current levels.

data.title: Rare Stock Picks In May 2025 - From 27 Discerning Analysts
data.description:
The rebound we mentioned in last month’s article continued in May, with the S&P 500 near the YTD highs. Are there any compelling investment ideas out there that may have flown under your radar?

Below is a list of May Buy recommendations made by analysts who had no other bullish recommendations over the past 3 months.

For your directory assistance, we've classified the opportunities by sector.

Healthcare

Alpha Cognition (ACOG) - Longtime analyst Anders Research says it is a relatively unknown pharmaceutical company that just launched its first commercial product, and appears poised to capitalize on a large and underserved ~7 million-patient Alzheimer’s market. - Alpha Cognition: Launching A Differentiated Drug Into A Large Alzheimer's Market Opportunity

Eledon Pharmaceuticals (ELDN) - Longtime analyst Daron Evans says its lead candidate shows promise in improving kidney function post-transplant, potentially becoming the new standard-of-care, and the upcoming Phase 2 study data in Q4 2025 is a critical milestone, with potential for transformative value. - Eledon: A Company With Good Early Data In Kidney Transplant Rejection And A Full Balance Sheet

uniQure (QURE) - Longtime analyst C. C. Abbott highlights a potential catalyst as its lead program for Huntington's Disease (which currently has no cure or disease-modifying treatment) is planning to file an application via the accelerated approval pathway, while the HD treatment space is estimated to reach $1.4B by 2034. - uniQure: A Buy For Their Lead In Huntington's Program, In Light Of PTC's Data (Editor’s note: Shares have rallied since the article was published)

Senseonics (SENS) - Longtime analyst Federico Cuneo says the risk/reward ratio seems skewed favorably, and the stock has the potential to be a multibagger investment, as it recently introduced a 365-day Continuous Glucose Monitoring system, aiming to disrupt a market dominated by short-term CGMs from DexCom, Abbott Laboratories, and Medtronic. - Revisiting Senseonics After 4 Years: Still A Big Investment Opportunity

Financials

Enova International (ENVA) - Value investor Eli Gooch says it is an overlooked and compelling opportunity given the rapid earnings growth while management's significant ownership and strong execution, along with growing software investments, suggest a developing competitive moat. - Enova International: Growing Too Fast To Ignore

eToro (ETOR) - Longtime analyst Nicholas Kitonyi says strong crypto-driven revenue growth and favorable U.S. regulatory shifts could propel its market cap toward $10B, while the platform's unique social trading features and multi-asset offerings provide a competitive edge over peers. - Why It's Not Too Late To Buy eToro

East West Bancorp (EWBC) - Value investor Bet Size Matters says that despite its strong track record, it trades at just 10x forward earnings while it maintains superior asset quality, conservative loan loss provisioning, and strong profitability metrics. - East West Bancorp: An Undervalued Bank With Durable Strength

Communications

iHeartMedia (IHRT) - Longtime analyst Matthew Smith says shares trade at a level that could lead to an asymmetric payoff for those investors willing to hold for an 18-36 month period, and management's growth initiatives and signs of national advertisers returning to the market suggest potential for solid full-year results. - iHeartMedia: Is A Turnaround Occurring?

CuriosityStream (CURI) - Longtime analyst Edward Schneider, CFA says it has rapidly transformed into a profitable, high-growth company with a scalable business, and the Street is not adapting to the pace of AI Content Licensing revenue growth, creating a buying opportunity. - CuriosityStream: Climbing The Wall Of Worry (Editor’s note: Shares have rallied since the article was published)

Cable One (CABO) - Longtime analyst Nils de Graaf says the significant drop over the past few years is a buying opportunity, its financing is secure, and the low valuation makes it an attractive takeover candidate. - Cable One: Cashcow And Takeover Candidate

IDT Corporation (IDT) - Individual investor Jonquil Capital says even after the rally (driven by substantial de-risking and consistent earnings growth), it is still cheap compared to its sum-of-the-parts value, with catalysts like SG&A reduction, a potential NRS spin-off, and AI-driven innovation. - IDT Corporation: Still Undervalued And Outperforming

Tripadvisor (TRIP) - Longtime analyst Daniel Shvartsman says its long-term story looks appealing and that even a conservative 10x EBITDA multiple results in >100% upside. - Questions Going Into Tripadvisor's Q1 Earnings

The Trade Desk (TTD) - Growth investor LDV Research says it is a market leader, and it continues to take market share, while the strong growth prospects and a higher multiple provides upside. - The Trade Desk: Business Momentum Ahead For This Category Leader

Alphabet (GOOG) - Longtime analyst Rob Barnett says it remains fundamentally undervalued - although digital advertising growth is moderating, GOOG maintains strong margins and market leadership while the Cloud division is gaining ground with improving profitability. - Alphabet: There Is Upside To Be Captured

Technology

RADCOM (RDCM) - Longtime analyst Cobiaman says despite strong fundamentals, it trades at a deep discount to peers, and the analyst expects a rerating as it adds new customers and sustains organic growth. - Radcom: Category Leader Growing At 18% At An 8.8x Cash P/E

Clearwater Analytics (CWAN) - Longtime analyst Donovan Jones is bullish given its robust growth prospects and strategic acquisitions, while it is well-positioned in the growing investment management software market. - Clearwater Analytics: Boosting Vertical Integration Through Recent Acquisitions (Upgrade)

Seagate Technology (STX) - Longtime analyst Kerrisdale Capital Management says it trades at just 11x 2026 earnings, far too low given its growth profile and secular tailwinds while it is well-managed, operating in an oligopolistic competitive landscape, with a track record of smart and shareholder-friendly capital allocation. - Seagate: It's HAMR Time

Consumer Discretionary

Watches of Switzerland (OTCPK:WOSGF) - Longtime analyst Investor Aide says it offers strong value with resilient financials, robust EBIT growth, and a compelling 17% ROCE, and its prime retail locations, and exclusive brand relationships support a bullish long-term outlook. - Watches of Switzerland: Dollar-Cost Averaging May Still Be The Order Of The Day For Luxury Retail Bulls

Whirlpool (WHR) - Individual investor Bela Lakos says traditional price multiples and a dividend discount model suggest WHR is undervalued and despite macroeconomic challenges, it expects top-line growth and improved free cash flow in 2025. - Whirlpool: Why We Believe The Stock Is A Buy After The Q1 Results

Toll Brothers (TOL) - Individual investor Mehmood P. Bakhsh says the luxury housing market recovery and effective cost and debt management strategies position Toll Brothers for significant growth and shareholder value creation in the medium to long term. - Toll Brothers Capital Efficient Growth Model Creates An Attractive Low-Risk, High Return Opportunity For Shareholders

Industrials

Global Industrial (GIC) - Individual investor Sanjeev Vaid says it offers a substantial margin of safety and that despite near-term economic headwinds and integration risks, Private Label growth and cost control can unlock significant upside. - Global Industrial: Still Undervalued Despite Recent Missteps

Sunrun (RUN) - Longtime analyst Seth Glucksman says the estimated book value, driven by contract assets, is significantly higher than market cap, while rising utility electricity rates give RUN room to expand margins and cover cost increases. - Don't Run From Sunrun: Why I'm Bullish (Editor’s note: Shares have fallen since the article was published)

Energy

Uranium Energy (UEC) -Longtime analyst ZMK Capital says it is uniquely positioned with U.S.-based assets, a large uranium stockpile, and zero debt, offering high leverage to uranium prices while global uranium demand is rising due to nuclear expansion and supply remains constrained. - Uranium Energy Corp: A Strategic Bet On Nuclear's Resurgence?

Vista Energy (VIST) - Value investor Alan Galecki says it offers superior growth, lower costs, and higher margins compared to Big Oil, making it a compelling alternative for energy sector investors. - Vista Energy: My Preferred Oil Stock Due To Key Competitive Advantages Against Big Oil

Utilities

Central Puerto (CEPU) - Longtime analyst Zach Schweinsberg says its expansion into renewables and mining, plus vast land holdings, positions it as a diversified, forward-thinking energy company in Argentina. - Central Puerto Positioned For Growth Amid Volatility

Engie (OTCPK:ENGIY) - Individual investor Ellsworth Research says the stock price weakness presents a buying opportunity given the long-term growth anticipated from new capacity and strategic investments. - Engie: Best Energy Transition Utility

Real Estate

Host Hotels & Resorts (HST) - Longtime analyst Philip Wang says it boasts one of the strongest balance sheets among lodging REITs, and it offers attractive income from the well-covered 5%+ dividend yield. - Host Hotels & Resorts: Fortress Balance Sheet, 5% Yield Make This A Buy

Inclusion Criteria: The above ideas were objectively compiled based on the set criteria. Exclusions include new Seeking Alpha analysts, broad market calls, crypto ideas, leveraged ETFs, and stocks that were repeat recommendations by the analyst.

Thanks for reading.

This account (Rare Stock Picks) will publish future iterations of this report in the upcoming months. If you missed our Rare Stock Picks from April, you can find it here.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: DocuSign: Why I Am Buying The Q1 Crash Hand Over Fist
data.description:
DocuSign (NASDAQ:DOCU) managed to beat top and bottom-line estimates for its first fiscal quarter on Thursday, but nonetheless, shares of the e-Signature leader dropped 17% in after-hours trading, mainly because of lower billings. I believe the market is massively overreacting to DocuSign’s earnings report for the first fiscal quarter and since the company continues to report decent net retention and remained highly profitable based off of free cash flow, investors may want to consider doubling down on DocuSign’s battered shares.

Previous rating

I rated shares of DocuSign a sell in my last work on the company in December 2024 – DocuSign: This Is The Perfect Time To Sell (Rating Downgrade) – as the SaaS firm generated strong results in its subscription business, but seemed to be overvalued. In the first quarter, DocuSign’s core business continued to expand, and the platforms remained high FCF-profitable. DocuSign's revenue guidance for Q2'26 is solid, and the company raised its stock buyback authorization by $1.0B in order to return more cash to shareholders going forward.

DocuSign beats earnings, continues to have strong momentum in subscriptions

The SaaS firm reported better-than-expected earnings on both the top and bottom line for Q1'26: DocuSign reported $0.90 per-share in adjusted earnings, which beat the consensus estimate by $0.09 per-share. The top line came in at $763.7M, which out-performed the average prediction by $14.7M.

DocuSign is the leader in the e-Signature market and as such the company has a strong offer for its mostly enterprise clients: DocuSign helps companies conclude agreements inside and outside the enterprise, and streamlines agreement processes through its technology. With its unique software products that allow for remote contract agreements, DocuSign has been able to build a profitable subscription business.

DocuSign generated $764M in revenue in the first quarter, showing 8% year-over-year growth. Of these revenues, $746M came from its underlying subscription business, which also saw 8% top-line growth in Q1'26. Therefore, 98% of the SaaS firm's total revenue comes in the form of subscriptions.

The subscription business is doing well for DocuSign, mainly because the company succeeds to sign on more enterprise customers to its document platform. DocuSign had 1.7M customers on its platform at the end of the April quarter, showing 10% year-over-year growth.

In terms of net retention, the company is doing slightly better than in the year-earlier period: net retention, which measures how much money customers are spending on the DocuSign platform from one reporting to the next, was 101%, showing 2 PP expansion year-over-year. A net retention figure below 100% signals potential organic revenue challenges and should therefore be considered as a potential warning sign that customer monetization is weakening.

What didn't look so great for DocuSign in Q1'26 were its billings which came in at $740M in Q1'26 with growth slowing down to 4%. However, the slowdown in billings does not necessarily indicate a slowdown in the company's business but rather may be related to companies taking more time to extend subscription plans. What matters more, in my opinion, is DocuSign's revenue guidance, and here the company is doing quite well.

Strong free cash flows and capital returns

DocuSign’s strong market position in the e-Signature market has led to considerable profitability for the company, and the SaaS enterprise has been consistently profitable in terms of free cash flows in the last year.

DocuSign generated $228M in free cash flow in the first quarter, which calculated to a free cash flow margin of 30% which compares to an average quarterly margin of % in the trailing-twelve-month period. The software company is increasingly focused on returning more cash to shareholders, which is why DocuSign announced on Thursday that it is raising its stock buyback authorization by $1.0B to $1.4B.

Free Cash Flow Margin 33% 27% 28% 36% 30% -

(Source: Author)

While DocuSign is growing its subscription business, the company experienced a slight drop-off in billings in the first quarter, which may explain why the company’s shares were brutalized after the Q1'26 earnings scorecard.

For the second quarter, DocuSign expects to generate between $777M and $781M in revenue, which calculates to 6% top-line growth at the mid-point. The outlook also came in ahead of Wall Street's estimate of $774.5M.

For FY 2026, DocuSign expects $3,151M to $3,161M in total revenue which means the company raised its outlook, at the mid-point, by $22M.

DocuSign’s valuation

DocuSign’s shares are not cheap, but the recent drop likely constitutes a buying opportunity as investors are overreacting to the company's billings situation. As a result, I believe shares of the e-Signature leader have considerable rebound potential once investors have digested the relatively moderate drop in billings that weighed so heavily on investor sentiment on Thursday.

Shares of DocuSign are currently valued at a price-to-revenue ratio – based off of FY 2026 expectations – of 5.6X, which is 37% above the firm's longer term, 3-year average P/S ratio. However, rival software companies like Microsoft (MSFT), Oracle (ORCL), and Adobe (ADBE) are trading at higher price-to-revenue ratios between 6.2X and 11.0X.

Microsoft is the most expensive company in the industry group, although it should be said that it owns the second-largest Cloud platform in the world, after Amazon (AMZN)'s AWS. Microsoft's shares have, however, revaluation potential because of the potential related to its Cloud (Azure) operations: Microsoft: A Strong Cloud Bet For 2025 (Rating Upgrade).

Excluding Microsoft, the industry group average P/S ratio is 6.2X. In my opinion, the massive 17% drop following the company's first-quarter earnings scorecard is wildly exaggerated and creates an engagement opportunity for growth investors.

If shares of DocuSign were to revalue to the industry group average revenue multiplier of 6.2X, the SaaS firm would have upside potential to an estimated fair value of $103 per share. Since DocuSign dropped to ~$77 in after-hours trading, shares have a massive 34% upside revaluation potential.

Risks with DocuSign

The biggest risk for DocuSign is that the company could be losing customers to other e-Signature companies, or potentially see weakening customer monetization through a drop-off in its net retention rate. What would change my mind about DocuSign is if the SaaS firm were to see a deterioration in its free cash flow profile or if the company were to see its net retention rate drop below 100%, indicating organic revenue challenges.

Final thoughts

DocuSign is growing at high single-digit rates on a consolidated top-line basis as well as it's in its core subscription business. Although billings dropped Q/Q and growth slowed, I see a healthy SaaS firm with high profitability and a raised revenue outlook for FY 2026. Now that DocuSign also increased its stock buyback authorization, the company is set to return more cash to shareholders, which makes the SaaS firm a capital return play. Shares of DocuSign are more attractively valued on the drop and investors seem to be overreacting to the company's billing situation, in my opinion. Since the outlook for Q2'26 shows solid top-line growth and the subscription business is also expanding at a good clip, I am changing my stock rating from sell to buy.

GDX is set to track a new index starting September 19, 2025. Here's a look at how the transition to the MarketVector Global Gold Miners Index may change its portfolio.

Later this year, the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (NYSEARCA:GDX) will begin tracking a new index. Since its inception, GDX has tracked the NYSE Arca Gold Miners Index. After the market closes on September 19, 2025, GDX will transition to the MarketVector Global Gold Miners Index (MVGDXTR).

data.title: EV Company News For The Month Of May 2025
data.description:
Welcome to the May 2025 edition of Electric Vehicle [EV] company news.

The past month saw another solid month of EV sales reported for April. YTD sales to end April are up 27% YoY, slightly ahead of the Trend Investing forecast of 25% YoY growth for 2025.

So far in 2025 (YTD to end April) Tesla Model Y remains the world's best selling BEV, with Tesla Model 3 still in 2nd place; however the BYD Seagull (Dolphin Mini) at 3rd place is gaining ground. Just behind these are the Geely Geome Xingyuan, Wuling HongGuang Mini, and the Xiaomi SU7.

In terms of top selling auto groups so far in 2025 BYD has a commanding lead, selling ~25% of all plugin electric cars globally, over double its nearest competitor (see chart below).

Plugin electric car sales as of end April 2025

Global plugin electric car sales were ~1.5 million in April 2025, up 22% on April 2024 sales. Global plugin electric car market share in April was 22% and 21% YTD (for 2025).

China plugin electric car sales were over 900,000 in April 2025, up over 30% on April 2024 sales. Plugin electric car market share in China for April was 52%, and 48% YTD (for 2025).

Europe plugin electric car sales were 282,000 in April 2025, up 28% YoY, reaching 26% market share and 25% YTD (for 2025). Norway reached 97.4% share, Sweden 63.3%, Germany 28.8%, France 25.3%, and UK 32.1% in April 2025.

USA plugin electric car sales were 131,642 (as reported by Argonne National Laboratory) in April 2025, up 4.8% on April 2024 sales. Plugin electric car market share in USA for April was 9.00%.

Australia plugin electric car sales market share was 9.3% for April 2025. New Zealand was at ~10%.

Note: Globally, 100% battery electric cars represented 66% market share YTD (as of end April 2025) of all plugin electric cars sold. The above sales include light commercial vehicles.

Note: An acknowledgement to CleanTechnica for their work compiling most of the electric car sales data quoted above and in the article below.

Top selling global plugin electric car groups YTD (as of end April 2025) (source)

We are still only at the extremely early stage of the EV boom - We have still only electrified < 6% of the global fleet of 1.475 billion, with a staggering ~94% still to go.

Trend Investing's global plugin electric car sales forecast to 2040 (green bars)

data.title: Dollar Better Bid Ahead Of Jobs Report As Japanese And German Data Disappoint, But Will It Be Sustained?
data.description:
Overview

It is not clear what happened yesterday, the first time US and Chinese leaders have spoken since the inauguration. The US readout suggests trade was only discussed, and a deal on the rare earths was reached. China's readout included an expression of concern about the US planned arms sales to Taiwan and the need for more talks to resolve the issue. The agreement in Geneva apparently covered bilateral actions, and Beijing's export controls on several critical minerals and magnets are universal. Still, more talks have been planned, even if not scheduled. Although the Trump-Musk break-up is as dramatic as one might expect, given the volatile personalities, the focus is on today's US employment data after a series of disappointing reports, including ADP and the weekly jobless claims. Still, the greenback is firmer against the G10 currencies, but mixed against the emerging market currencies, where the euro's pullback is a drag on central European currencies.

Equities were mixed in the Asia Pacific region. South Korea's Kospi was the strongest with a 1.5% gain and a 5.3% rise on the week as election offers a chance for political stability after the turmoil earlier this year. Europe's Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is flat as it tries to extend its advance for the fourth consecutive session. US index futures are 035%-0.50% higher as they recoup part of yesterday's losses. Bond yields are softer. Poor household spending data from Japan weighed on rate, and even at the very long-end of the curve. Disappointing Germany industrial production and export figures helped drag European bond yields 2-4 bps lower. The 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) is near 4.37%, slightly lower on the day and a little more than six basis points lower on the week, rivalling the 10-year Gilt for the best performance this week. Gold is consolidating quietly within yesterday's range and is trading so far today mostly between $3353 and $3375. It settled slightly below $3290 last week. July WTI remains in the upper end of Monday's trading range, which extended to almost $64. It settled on Monday near $62.50 and has mostly held above it. Today's range is roughly $62.80-$63.35.

USD: The Dollar Index is recovering from yesterday's decline, which brought it to around 98.35, its lowest level since April 22. It was initially sold in the response to ECB's Lagarde's observation, which the swaps market had already concluded, that the ECB easing cycle is nearly over. However, news that Trump and Xi spoke and seemed to have overcome what appears to be a misunderstanding lifted DXY to around 98.85, a little above Wednesday's low (~98.65). Follow-through buying today is probing the 99.00 area. It may take a move above the 99.40 area to lift the technical tone. Barring a new social media post by the president, the US employment data is the key ahead of the weekend. The consensus sees slower job growth in May. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey is for 130k increase after a 177k rise April. The average through the first four months of the year is 144k, down from 176k average in the same period last year. The market may be more sensitive to a change in the unemployment rate, which stood at 4.2% in both March and April. It was at 4.0% last May. The JOLTS report (April) showed an unexpected rise in job openings, led by the private sector, while openings in manufacturing and leisure and hospitality fell. On the other hand, the ADP private sector estimate for May was dismal at 37k, a third of what was expected). It was the lowest estimate since the fluke -53k loss in March 2023. Although the short-term divergence between the BLS estimate and ADP can be stark, what some news wires call the "whisper number" is bound to be lower. A weak report today, and especially a rise in the unemployment rate, could see the market push the next Fed cut back into Q3. Alongside the gradual slowing of the labor market, and several elevated measures of consumer debt stress, consumer credit slowed in Q1 25 (to just less than $20 bln) from more than $30 bln in Q4 24 and $25 bln in Q1 24. April's figures will be released today, but typically do not move the markets.

Euro: The "hawkish cut" by the ECB lifted the euro to almost $1.1500 yesterday from a low on Wednesday around $1.1360. On the pullback on Trump-Xi talks, the single currency found new bids near $1.1430. It has pulled back to almost $1.1410 today, partly in response to disappointing German data. Support extends toward $1.1380. Although April factory orders were yesterday rose by 0.6% instead of a decline by 1.5% as economists polled by Bloomberg expected, industrial production fell by more than forecast (-1.4% vs. -1.0%) and the March series was revised to show a 2.3% increase instead of 3.0%. And April exports fell by 1.7%, which was also more than anticipated. Yet, on the eurozone level, growth in Q1 was twice as strong as initially reported (0.6% vs. 0.3%). The swaps market went into the meeting anticipating a year-end rate slightly above 1.60% and rose eight basis points by the end of the session.

CNY: The dollar held above the seven-month low against the offshore yuan seen in late May near CNH7.1615) and recovered to about CNH7.1750 before stalling yesterday. Today, it has taken out yesterday's high (~CNH7.1820) and risen to about CNH7.1870. The price action over the past few weeks may be carving out a new range between CNH7.16 and CNH7.23. The PBOC set the dollar's reference rate at CNY7.1845 (CNY7.1865 yesterday and CNY7.1848 last Friday). China reports May CPI and PPI early Monday. The market expected deflationary forces still grip prices. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey sees consumer prices falling by 0.2% year-over-year after falling 0.1% in April. Consumer prices turned down in February after having been slowly rising since January 2024. Although many focus on weak demand (though presented as a percentage of GDP, which, we argue, reflects continued strong investment) the drag on goods prices comes from food. Core prices, excluding food and energy, has been mostly positive, though they did slip in February below zero. It stood at 0.5% in April year-over-year. Note that earlier this week, Switzerland reported that on the harmonized EU methodology, its CPI is -0.2% year-over-year. However, the Swiss 10-year yield is around 0.25%, while China's is hovering near 1.70%. China's producer prices are expected to have fallen 3.0% year-over-year after a 2.7% decline in April. If so, that would match the deepest deflation since July 2023 and the third consecutive decline. It had appeared to be stabilizing in the previous four months.

JPY: The dollar has chopped back and forth between around JPY142.40 and JPY144.40 this week. It is making session highs in the European morning near JPY144.20. Still, it may require a close above JPY144.80 to signal a breakout. Japan's household spending was a major disappointment. Rather than rise 1.5% as the economists projected, it fell by 0.1%. The average year-over-year gain in Q1 of 0.8% was the best quarterly performance since Q3 22. Yet in GDP terms, consumer spending rose by 0.2% in Q1 25 after a 0.3% increase in Q4 24. The Q1 GDP estimate of a 0.2% contraction quarter-over-quarter will be updated early Monday.

GBP: Sterling rose to a new three-year high yesterday near $1.3615. It held in better than the euro, and on the pullback to only around $1.3575 bids re-emerged. It is holding below $1.3600 today, and tested support in the $1.3530 area in the European morning. A break of it could see $1.3490-$1.3500. A close above $1.3575-80 would appear constructive. The UK-US trade deal seemed to allow British steel and aluminum into the US without the levy the US re-introduced, provided that its supply chain was clean (i.e., not re-exporting Chinese product). Yet, it turns out that UK steel and aluminum will be subject to the 25% tariff announced last week after the US agreed to allow US Steel to be combined with Nippon Steel. The UK also seems vulnerable if the final version of the US budget proposal contains Section 899, which retaliates against companies operating in the US whose home country pursues policies that the US does not like, such as a digital tax or implementing the OCED's Pillar Two, (sets15% minimum corporate tax rate on large companies and allows others to top it off if it is under-taxed at home).

CAD: The Canadian dollar made a new high for the year yesterday after Ottawa reported a record-goods trade deficit as exports to the US collapsed by almost 16%. The greenback reached CAD1.3635 before recovering toward CAD1.3675. It has extended the recovered to almost around CAD1.3685, but the CAD1.3700 area looks more important. A move above there could target CAD1.3750. After the Bank of Canada's pause on Wednesday, with overnight target rate at 2.75%, which is within estimates of the neutral range, attention turns to the labor market today. Canada's labor market has slowed considerably in recent months. The average monthly jobs growth in the first four months of 2025 is about 13k, down from almost 42k a month in the Jan-Apr 2024 period. The median forecast tin Bloomberg's survey is for a 12.5k loss of jobs last month. Canada has lost an average of 3.75k full time positions a month this year, and grew 25k a month in the same period last year. The unemployment rate has been steadily rising since bottoming at 5% in late 2022 and again in early 2023. It was at 5.7% in January 2024 and finished the year at 6.7%. Canada's unemployment rate stood at 6.9% in April, matching the cyclical high. It is expected to have risen to 7.0% last month.

AUD: According to Bloomberg's pricing, the Australian dollar made a new seven-month high yesterday by 1/100 of a cent to nearly $0.6540.The technical objective we have been emphasizing was the (61.8%) retracement of the Australian dollar's decline from last October's high (~$0.6940) to April's low (~$0.5915) found near $0.6550. It settled above $0.6500 for the first time this year, but was unable to sustain the momentum and has pulled back to about $0.6485 today. A band of support may be found between $0.6455 and $0.6475.

MXN: The dollar reached a new eight-month low against the peso today, slightly below MXN19.14. The MXN19.00-MXN19.05 offers the next important chart area. It has been a gradual but persistent grind lower in recent weeks. And that is the point. The relatively low volatility, liquidity, and attractive rates make it an ideal for carry-trades against the dollar. Its actual volatility over the past month is around 8.9% (annualized). One can earn more carry in Brazil, but the volatility is almost 50% higher than the peso, and the liquidity is worse. One of the implications of this is that a firm CPI print on Monday, with both the headline and core rates moving above the top of the target range that extends to 4%, the peso's attractiveness may be enhanced rather than diminished.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CoreWeave: Benefiting From AI Workload Growth; Initiate With 'Buy'
data.description:
CoreWeave (CRWV) is an AI Hyperscaler, offering proprietary software and cloud services for enterprise customers through its CoreWeave Cloud Platform. I like the company’s unique value proposition in the rapidly growing AI hyperscaler market. I am initiating with a ‘Buy’ rating with a fair value of $191 per share.

I think CoreWeave’s competitive advantages can be summarized as follows:

  • As disclosed in its IPO prospectus, the company operated 32 data centers running with more than 250,000 GPUs as of December 31, 2024. I think these active purpose-built data centers could enable the company to serve a wide range of enterprise customers, providing essential AI workloads and computing power for everything from training to inference use cases. It is relatively easy for enterprise customers to establish long-term contracts with CoreWeave for AI computing capabilities, compared to DIY.
  • As depicted in the slide below, CoreWeave offers a holistic suite of solutions for AI training and inference computing needs, including cloud ops, lifecycle management, observability, networking and storage solutions on top of its data center infrastructure layer. Over the past few years, CoreWeave has built robust relationships with key industry players in the observability, security, storage, chips as well as networking industries, which could enable CoreWeave to build a comprehensive AI hyperscaler environment that integrates both proprietary technologies as well as best-in-class third-party services and solutions.

  • Typically, enterprises need multiple years to fully integrate and utilize CoreWeave's AI infrastructure, as detailed in the slide below. These multi-year contracts require ongoing technology support from CoreWeave and involve tremendous data and workload migrations, making CoreWeave’s business highly sticky. Due to high migration costs and extended timeline, it would be hard for CoreWeave’s customers to terminate their long-term contracts. In other words, CoreWeave’s business is highly recurring in nature. As the company disclosed, long-term contracts represent around 96% of total revenue, which is quite remarkable.

  • I think CoreWeave has a huge total addressable markets [TAM], and in the IPO prospectus, the company estimated their total TAM for AI inference/fine-tuning, AI workload monitoring, and training infrastructure will grow at a CAGR of 38% from 2023 to 2028, reaching around $330 billion related to training infrastructure. Indeed, CoreWeave has a huge runway for future growth, driven by market expansion fueled by AI workloads, assuming the company continues to expand their data center presents and integrates more solutions and software into their CoreWeave Cloud Platform.
  • Lastly, the management plans to expand their AI cloud platform into some new industries, including highly regulated sectors such as banks and healthcare industries. It is evident that technology and communication services companies are the early adopters of AI cloud platform, and I think traditional industries are gradually migrating their workloads to AI cloud infrastructure. Therefore, CoreWeave has huge potentials to expand its cloud infrastructure into these highly regulated and traditional industries, which could potentially accelerate its business growth.

CoreWeave released its Q1 FY25 result on May 14, posting 420% revenue growth and 550% adjusted operating income growth. As depicted in the chart below, CoreWeave has experienced explosive business growth over the past few quarters driven by new customers wins and rapid growth of AI workloads.

My key takeaway from the quarter was the $11.9 billion contract signed with OpenAI in March 2025. OpenAI will acquire $350.0 million of CoreWeave stock through a new issuance. I think the partnership with OpenAI validates that CoreWeave’s AI ability to satisfy the complex demands of advanced AI machine learning. I have no doubt that CoreWeave’s technology and platform could be potentially adopted by large enterprises customers in the future. During the Q1 FY25 earnings call, the management disclosed a recent $4 billion contract with another large AI enterprise. I anticipate CoreWeave will continue to win more deals in the near future.

For FY25, the company is guiding for $4.9-$5 billion in revenue with $800-$830 million in adjusted operating income, as detailed in the slide below.

Despite CoreWeave’s explosive growth, the company is only anticipated to achieve around $5 billion in revenue for FY25. As discussed previously, their multi-year contracts have a long lead time from initial agreement to the full implementation. As such, I think CoreWeave is in the infant stage of business expansion. I forecast the company’s revenue will grow by 165% in FY25, aligned with the company’s guidance. Due to the long-term nature of their contracts, I think the company has great visibilities for near-term revenue growth. I anticipate the revenue growth will slow down to 80% in FY26, then decelerate to 50% from FY27 to FY28, followed by 30% normalized revenue growth from FY29 onwards. If the overall AI training infrastructure reaches $330 billion in FY28, as the management anticipated, I calculate CoreWeave’s revenue will only represent slightly over 5% of the total market by FY28, which I consider quite reasonable.

I anticipate 10bps annual margin expansion. As the company is investing heavily in CAPEX, I forecast their depreciation costs will grow significantly over the next few years, which will cause challenges for their margin expansion. I calculate the rising depreciation costs will bring 30bps operating margin pressures for the company, while the reduction in SG&A expenses will generate 40bps of operating leverage. All in all, I forecast the company will deliver 10bps annual operating margin expansion.

DCF summary:

FCFF:

As the company generates negative free cash flow, I anticipate the company will borrow $15 billion of new debts to fund its business growth in the next five years. WACC is calculated to be 10.1% assuming: risk-free 4.2%; beta 1.3; equity risk premium 6%; cost of debt 5%; equity $72 bn; debt $23 bn; tax rate 15%. I set the terminal growth to 5%, aligned with the overall industry growth. Discounting all the future FCFF and subtracting net debt, the fair value is calculated to be $191 per share, as detailed in the table below:

Downside Risks

  • CoreWeave’s top 2 customers accounted for around 77% of total revenue in FY24, with Microsoft (MSFT) being the largest at 62% of total revenue. As such, CoreWeave has significant customer concentration risk and their growth is largely decided by Microsoft’s cloud infrastructure expansion. However, as CoreWeave tries to expand their services to other Hyperscalers and enterprise customers, I’d anticipate the concentration risks will gradually diminish.
  • Due to CoreWeave’s substantial CAPEX spendings, I anticipate CoreWeave will need to raise debts or issue more equities in the near future. As discussed previously, the company plans to spend $20-$23 billion in CAPEX for FY25, a quite high level compared to its potential cash flow from operations. If CoreWeave raises more capital via stock offerings in the future, their stock price might face near-term pressure.
  • Lastly, to sustain its competitive advantages, CoreWeave needs to continue high spendings in data center expansions and GPU purchases. CoreWeave relies on Nvidia (NVDA) for key GPUs to its overall infrastructure build-out. As such, the relationship between Nvidia and CoreWeave could affect the availability of GPU products. In addition, the cloud Hyperscalers, like Microsoft, Amazon (AMZN) and Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), might increase their own AI cloud infrastructure build-outs, which will increase competition in the AI cloud infrastructure market.

Conclusion

CoreWeave’s holistic AI infrastructure solution will benefit from the massive growth in AI training and AI inference workloads, in my view. I am initiating with a ‘Buy’ rating with a fair value of $191 per share.

data.title: Largest U.S. Bank Stocks Carry The Sector In May
data.description:
The US banks with the biggest market capitalizations outperformed the rest of the sector in May.

The median total return in May was 3.6% for the 212 banks in an S&P Global Market Intelligence analysis. This market performance was lifted by the 16 banks with a market cap greater than $10 billion as of May 30. The largest-cap group had a 7.8% median total return, ranging from 4.0% at First Citizens BancShares Inc. (FCNCA) to 11.0% at Citigroup Inc.

Less than 10% of the banks in the analysis had a negative monthly return. Archbold, Ohio-based Farmers & Merchants Bancorp Inc. (OTCQX:FMCB); Harrisburg, Pennsylvania-based Mid Penn Bancorp Inc. (MPB); and Hammond, Louisiana-based First Guaranty Bancshares Inc. (FGBI) were the worst market performers, trading down 9.0%, 7.6% and 5.9%, respectively. The only bank with a market cap greater than $2 billion that lost ground in the market was Flagstar Financial Inc. (FLG), which recorded a return of negative 1.6% in May after a 25.6% return through the first four months of the year.

Least expensive banks

Dallas-based First Foundation Inc. (FFWM) was the least expensive bank in the analysis by price-to-adjusted tangible book value (TBV) for the fifth consecutive month. Its valuation was 46.9% as of May 30 and would have been higher if preferred shares from a capital raise in July 2024 were converted to common stock. The conversion would have lowered the bank's basic TBV to $9.42 from $11.77 at March 31, according to an April 30 investor presentation.

First Foundation recorded net income of $6.9 million for the first quarter after posting net losses for the previous two quarters. The bank's net interest margin has risen for four consecutive quarters, and management is projecting continued expansion. With a return to profitability and a smaller balance sheet, First Foundation's capital ratios increased across the board on a quarter-over-quarter basis.

Three analysts, previously at Wedbush Securities, joined Jefferies this year and initiated coverage on a basket of US banks, including First Foundation at "buy." The analysts also started covering Bethesda, Maryland-based Eagle Bancorp Inc. (EGBN), ranked No. 2 by lowest price-to-adjusted TBV, with a "hold" rating and No. 18 Los Angeles-based Banc of California Inc. (BANC) with a "buy" recommendation.

Eagle Bancorp and First Internet Bancorp (INBK), which was the third-least expensive bank in the analysis, are two of the largest US banks that exceeded the 2006 commercial real estate regulatory guidance as of March 31.

Melville, New York-based First of Long Island Corp. (FLIC), which was the No. 8 bank by lowest valuation, will no longer be in the analysis as it was acquired by Englewood Cliffs, New Jersey-based ConnectOne Bancorp Inc. (CNOB) at the beginning of June.

Primis Financial Corp. (FRST), the 13th-cheapest bank, reported a $24.6 million gain in the first quarter from the deconsolidation of Panacea Financial Holdings. The McLean, Virginia-based bank's return on average tangible common equity jumped to 36.55% from 3.84% in the year-ago quarter.

In May, No. 15 Citigroup (C) announced the sale of its consumer banking business in Poland, as well as the sale of Citi Global Alternatives LLC. Additionally, Berkshire Hathaway Inc. disclosed that it exited its Citigroup stake of 14.6 million shares during the first quarter.

Most expensive banks

Abilene, Texas-based First Financial Bankshares Inc. (FFIN) ascended to the top valuation rank as of the end of May. Its price-to-adjusted TBV was 358.7%, up from 340.9% at April 30.

Dewitt, New York-based Community Financial System Inc. (CBU) slotted in at No. 2 following three consecutive months as the most expensive bank in the analysis.

The third-most expensive bank, Pathward Financial Inc. (CASH), received a noncompliance notice from Nasdaq Stock Market LLC for failing to file a Form 10-Q for the fiscal quarter ended March 31. Pathward's total return in May ran counter to the industry trend at negative 1.7%.

The No. 4 bank, The Bancorp Inc. (TBBK), announced that a deal to sell a problem real estate asset failed to close even after multiple deadline extensions.

On May 21, Oklahoma City-based BancFirst Corp. (BANF), the seventh-most expensive bank, announced its planned acquisition of Collinsville, Oklahoma-based American Bank of Oklahoma.

On May 29, No. 20 Wells Fargo & Co. (WFC) announced that it agreed to sell the assets of its rail equipment leasing business for $4.4 billion. On June 3, Wells Fargo (WFC) disclosed that the Federal Reserve removed the $1.95 trillion asset cap that was established for the bank.

Original Post

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Encouraging Q1 From Planet Labs Marks First Positive Operating Cash Flow
data.description:
Planet Labs' (NYSE:PL) share price has moved around quite a bit of late with earnings, and while it got appealingly low-priced when it dropped on Q4 earnings, I maintained my hold rating in March because the miss concerned me a little too. Now we have a Q1 earnings for the new fiscal year, and it's a lot more promising, but…

The price is up substantially Thursday on that estimate beat, which puts us back in the situation where, apart from Q4, Planet Labs has beat estimates every quarter for years. We were wondering if Q4 was a one-time issue, and these new earnings suggest it probably was, and the company is likely back on track.

Which means once again we're going to be looking over Planet Labs, seeing what's changed, what the numbers look like with the new figures, and the important accomplishment they beat on operating cash flow this quarter.

Q1: A Good Beat on Both EPS and Revenue

As mentioned, Planet Labs came in with a better than estimated earnings result, breaking even on a non-GAAP basis instead of the 3¢ loss they were anticipating. The beat was further encouraging because they also beat estimates on revenue by a little over $4 million. It doesn't sound like a lot, but Planet Labs is still a relatively small growth company, and growth that's a little bit faster can add up to a consequential improvement in the long run, especially with so many contracts of the business recurring. The most important, I think, is the gross margin.

That Planet Labs has been able to grow both its revenue and its gross margin year over year is an excellent trend, and reaching break-even, at least on a non-GAAP basis, is still a worthy milestone for a company that's still in the process of turning into a mature business.

That said, the estimate for the rest of the quarters of this fiscal year are for narrow non-GAAP losses, so we can't necessarily declare that threshold permanently crossed. Planet Labs' history of beating estimates though, and its improving margins, could mean they're getting closer and closer to the point where break-even is no longer a shock as it was this time.

Growing in Some Sectors

Not every sector of Planet Labs grew, however. In the conference call, they reported a slight year-over-year decline in the Civil Government sector, the result of a shorter contract happening to expire this quarter. They grew overall though, with the Defense and Intelligence sector showing the best results, up 20% year over year. That drove the overall growth, while the Commercial sector also stabilized after recent struggles.

Planet Labs is a relatively small company still, but they are in a lot of different market and government segments with their offerings. Government spending working the way it does, it may well continue to be the case that various political priorities favor some sectors over others at any given time, but Planet Labs appears to cover all such sectors pretty effectively, attracting new contracts here and there on all sorts of efforts.

Just recently, Planet Labs announced a new "7 figure" contract with the German Interior Ministry, and expanded a resource management agreement with the government of Wales. So while Civil Government seemed weak in Q1, such deals are being made and as they start to ramp up, it should mean that sector will perform well too.

As new, better satellites are put into orbit, Planet Labs can improve the quality of their offerings and potential open up new opportunities that weren't there before. Parsing all that data is complicated, and that's where AI comes in.

Planet Labs Remains Enthusiastic About AI

Last time we looked at Planet Labs, we talked a little about their partnership with Anthropic. That's still ongoing, but perhaps the more exciting thing is that they are working with Google AI on their Geospatial Reasoning effort.

A lot of the data Planet Labs' satellites necessarily collect in the course of their travel aren't used for much at present, only very specific images from very specific areas are often sold. Yet, that doesn't mean that data is ultimately worthless, as with AI parsing through it, noticing subtle changes in places that otherwise wouldn't be deserving of attention, the hope is that they'll be able to uncover even more insights, and Planet Labs, being the source of the data, could profit from what used to be unimportant side images.

Anthropic is a smaller, though relatively successful AI company, so it isn't necessarily a bad partner for Planet Labs to work with. Google is plainly much bigger in everything they do, though, so any relationship Planet Labs can have with them probably will directly lead to a more substantial result in pretty short order. It's not clear when this study by Google AI will lead to anything particularly accretive, but if Google is happy with Planet Labs' role in it, it could open up countless additional Commercial sector ventures.

I've always suspected Commercial would end up being the big sector for Planet Labs, but so far, they've defied my expectation with substantial government business growth. In the long run though, profitable Commercial deals could be even bigger, and Google is a step in that direction.

The Cash Flow First

Planet Labs is still not GAAP profitable, but that doesn't mean they're bleeding cash anymore, or heavily reliant on dilutive share offerings to keep the doors open. Q1 was the first time that they were operating cash flow positive, and relatively substantially so.

(source: most recent 10-Q from SEC)

So operating cash flow is a huge positive, obviously, but the lack of that huge investing cash flow resulting from new share issues is no small matter either. The 2025 number includes some $43 million in sales of securities, while the 2026 number includes only $582,000.

Operating cash flow this past quarter was not only positive, it was around double the company's CapEx, and with the highest gross margin and more and more contracts being reported all the time, they may be able to remain at or near cash flow positive in the future.

Q2 and Other Pitfalls

So things are looking really great after Q1, but I don't want to give the impression that it's all sunshine and rainbows from here. Planet Labs still has some things to contend with, not the least of which being that they're probably not going to maintain break-even status next quarter.

During the conference call, Planet Labs reported that they expect substantially higher CapEx in Q2. That's not an increase in guidance on CapEx for the full year, just that more of it will be coming up in the second quarter, and subsequently will be a drag on next quarter's earnings.

They might overcome this timing if they continue growing the gross margin, but that's not so easy. They offered guidance on non-GAAP gross margin of 56%-57%, which was slightly down from the 59% they reported this quarter. The general trend toward improving margins is a good sign, but it's not a straight shot, and it might not end up as high as it was in the past quarter.

All of this means that break-even, even on a non-GAAP basis, is likely to remain the exception rather than the rule for the time being.

A Buy but Only at the Right Price

What does all that add up to? I'm liking Planet Labs' prospects a lot more after Q1, but I think we should also temper are expectations, especially after the price went up so much on Thursday. I'm going to upgrade it to a buy, but with the caveat that it's only a buy at the right price and if you've got a long-term view.

Planet Labs might well pull back some from the price it is right now, so being patient and looking for a better entry point could turn out to be a good move. Likewise, prices seem to be really sensitive to quarterly earnings of late, so you shouldn't buy unless you're comfortable holding for a few years, as you can't always count on estimate beats as a catalyst.

Investors should watch the revenue growth and the acquisition of new contracts, as they still need to grow from where they are right now to reach dependable profitability. They can do it, certainly, but there are a lot of unknowns right now in the process, as this is a relatively new sector. I'd watch margins too because the growth is a very good sign, but keep in mind, as mentioned above, they probably won't continue to grow margins every single quarter.

Q1 shows Planet Labs to be on the right track, and if you can get in for a reasonable price, I believe it's worth exploring at this point.
